prompt:
So configparser's ExtendedInterpolation has burned us twice now (silent weirdness with $$ and useless errors when a ref chain loops), and honestly we only use a tiny slice of it. I want our own loader we fully control: `iniconf.py` with `load_ini(text)` that returns a plain dict-of-dicts, everything resolved, all values strings. Errors raise `IniError` (make it a ValueError subclass so old call sites keep working).

Syntax we actually use: `[section]` headers, `key = value` split at the FIRST equals (values may contain '='), whitespace trimmed off keys and values, empty value is fine and means "". Full-line comments start with ';' or '#'. Blank lines ignored. Duplicate key in a section: last one wins. Same section header appearing twice: merge. A key before any section header, a line without '=', or a `[section` missing its bracket are syntax errors, and the message MUST include the 1-based line number (we grep logs for these).

Interpolation: `${key}` pulls from the same section, `${section.key}` from anywhere (split at the first dot). Refs can chain multiple hops and can point at keys defined later in the file — order in the file must not matter for resolution. `$$` is a literal dollar and must never combine with what follows into a reference; a lone `$` that isn't `$$` or `${` just passes through. `${}` or an unterminated `${` is an IniError. A reference to a key that doesn't exist is an IniError naming the fully qualified missing key (like `s.nope`).

Cycles: detect them, don't stack-overflow. Report the whole chain in the message with ' -> ' separators, starting from the first key (in file order) whose resolution enters the loop, e.g. `interpolation cycle: a.x -> b.y -> a.x`. Note a diamond (two refs to the same key) is not a cycle — we hit that false positive with a homegrown loader at my last job.

Acceptance contract is test_iniconf.py, run as `python3 test_iniconf.py`. Don't import configparser, don't touch the tests.
